q: What is the primary function of the cardiovascular system?
a: Transport of materials to and from all parts of the body.

q: Give examples of materials entering the body that are transported by the cardiovascular system.
a: Oxygen is transferred from lungs to all cells. Nutrients and water are transferred from the intestinal tract to all cells.

q: Give examples of materials moved from cell to cell by the cardiovascular system.
a: Wastes are transferred from some cells to the liver for processing. Immune cells, antibodies, and clotting proteins are present in blood continuously and available to any cell that needs them. Hormones are transferred from endocrine cells to target cells. Stored nutrients are transferred from liver and adipose tissue to all cells.

q: Give examples of materials leaving the body that are transported by the cardiovascular system.
a: Metabolic wastes are transferred from all cells to the kidneys for elimination. Heat is transferred from all cells to the skin for dissipation. Carbon dioxide is transferred from all cells to the lungs for elimination.

q:
Describe the organization of the cardiovascular system, starting and ending in the aorta. 

[Image of the human cardiovascular system schematic]
a: The aorta branches into smaller arteries to distribute oxygenated blood to capillary networks in tissues. Blood returns from the upper and lower body via the superior and inferior vena cava into the right atrium. Blood moves to the right ventricle and is sent through pulmonary arteries to the lungs for oxygenation. Well-oxygenated blood returns through pulmonary veins to the left atrium, moves to the left ventricle, and is pumped back out into the aorta.

q: What is the location of capillaries?
a: Microscopic vessels in tissues.

q: What is the function of capillaries?
a: Site where blood exchanges material with the interstitial fluid.

q: Define arteries.
a: Blood vessels that carry blood away from the heart.

q: Define veins.
a: Blood vessels that return blood to the heart.

q: What is the location of the septum?
a: Central wall of the heart.

q: What is the function of the septum?
a: Divides the heart into independent left and right halves and prevents blood mixing.

q: What is the location of the atria?
a: Upper chambers of the heart.

q: What is the function of the atria?
a: Receives blood returning to the heart from the blood vessels.

q: What is the location of the ventricle?
a: Lower chambers of the heart.

q: What is the function of the ventricle?
a: Pumps blood out into the blood vessels.

q: What is the location of the pulmonary arteries?
a: Connect right ventricle to lungs.

q: What is the function of the pulmonary arteries?
a: Carry low-oxygen blood to the lungs.

q: What is the location of the pulmonary veins?
a: Connect lungs to left atrium.

q: What is the function of the pulmonary veins?
a: Carry well-oxygenated blood from the lungs to the left heart.

q: Define pulmonary circulation.
a: The blood vessels that go from the right ventricle to the lungs and back to the left atrium.

q: Define systemic circulation.
a: The blood vessels that carry blood from the left side of the heart to the tissues and back to the right side of the heart.

q: What is the location of the aorta?
a: Large artery exiting the left ventricle.

q: What is the function of the aorta?
a: Branches into smaller arteries to distribute oxygenated blood to capillary networks in tissues.

q: What is the location of the superior vena cava?
a: Vein entering the right atrium.

q: What is the function of the superior vena cava?
a: Returns blood from the upper part of the body.

q: What is the location of the inferior vena cava?
a: Vein entering the right atrium.

q: What is the function of the inferior vena cava?
a: Returns blood from the lower part of the body.

q: What is the location of the pericardium?
a: Membranous sac encasing the heart.

q: What is the function of the pericardium?
a: Protects the heart and contains a thin layer of pericardial fluid that lubricates the external surface as it beats.

q: What is the location of the myocardium?
a: Thick muscular walls of the heart.

q: What is the function of the myocardium?
a: Cardiac muscle responsible for contraction.

q: What is the location of the atrioventricular (AV) valves?
a: Between the atria and ventricles.

q: What is the function of the atrioventricular (AV) valves?
a: Ensure one-way flow from atria to ventricles and prevent backward flow.

q: What is the location of the semilunar valves?
a: Between the ventricles and the major arteries.

q: What is the function of the semilunar valves?
a: Snap closed when blood attempts to flow back into ventricles from arteries.

q: What is the location of the papillary muscles?
a: Moundlike extensions of ventricular muscle.

q: What is the function of the papillary muscles?
a: Tether chordae tendineae and provide stability, but do not actively open or close valves.

q: Describe the tricuspid valve.
a: It is the right AV valve that separates the right atrium and right ventricle, and it has three flaps.

q: Describe the bicuspid (mitral) valve.
a: It is the left AV valve that separates the left atrium and left ventricle, and it has two flaps.

q: What is the location of the aortic valve?
a: Between left ventricle and aorta.

q: What is the location of the pulmonary valve?
a: Between right ventricle and pulmonary trunk.

q: What is the location of the coronary circulation?
a: Branches originating from the aorta.

q: What is the function of the coronary circulation?
a: Arteries that nourish the heart muscle itself. Blood flows into capillaries, then coronary veins, and empties directly into the right atrium at the coronary sinus.

q: Define contractile cells and their function.
a: Typical striated muscle cells organized into sarcomeres that generate the mechanical force of the heart.

q: Define autorhythmic cells (pacemakers) and their function.
a: Specialized, anatomically distinct cells that generate action potentials spontaneously. The signal for contraction is myogenic.

q: Explain the key role of pacemaker cells in initiating action potentials.
a: They generate action potentials spontaneously without innervation because their signal for contraction is myogenic.

q: What is the first step in the process of EC-coupling within cardiac muscle?
a: An action potential enters a contractile cell from an adjacent cell via gap junctions.

q: What happens after the action potential enters the t-tubules during EC-coupling?
a: It opens voltage-gated L-type calcium channels, allowing calcium to enter the cell down its electrochemical gradient.

q: Define Calcium-induced calcium release (CICR).
a: Calcium entry opens ryanodine receptor (RyR) calcium release channels in the sarcoplasmic reticulum, causing stored calcium to flow out into the cytosol.

q: How is a large calcium signal created during cardiac EC coupling?
a: Multiple local calcium sparks sum together, with 90% coming from the SR and 10% from extracellular fluid.

q: What is the final step initiating contraction in cardiac EC coupling?
a: Calcium diffuses and binds to troponin, initiating crossbridge formation and sliding filament contraction.

q: What is the first step in the process of cardiac muscle relaxation?
a: Cytoplasmic calcium concentration decreases, causing calcium to unbind from troponin.

q: What happens mechanically after calcium unbinds from troponin during relaxation?
a: Myosin releases actin, and the filaments slide back to their relaxed position.

q: Describe the role of the calcium-ATPase in cardiac muscle relaxation.
a: It transports calcium back into the sarcoplasmic reticulum.

q: Define the Na+-Ca2+ exchanger (NCX) antiporter and its function in relaxation.
a: It is a membrane protein that removes calcium from the cell by moving 1 Ca2+ out against its gradient for every 3 Na+ moving in down their gradient.

q: What is the role of the sodium-potassium pump (Na+-K+-ATPase) in cardiac muscle relaxation?
a: It pumps the Na+ that entered via the NCX back out of the cell to maintain gradients.

q: Describe Phase 4 of an action potential in cardiac contractile cells.
a: Phase 4 is the resting membrane potential, which is stable at about -90 mV.

q: Describe Phase 0 of an action potential in cardiac contractile cells.
a: Phase 0 is depolarization. A wave of depolarization moves through gap junctions, voltage-gated Na+ channels open, and Na+ rapidly enters, bringing the potential to +20 mV.

q: Describe Phase 1 of an action potential in cardiac contractile cells.
a: Phase 1 is initial repolarization. Na+ channels close, fast K+ channels open, and K+ begins to leave the cell.

q: Describe Phase 2 of an action potential in cardiac contractile cells.
a: Phase 2 is the plateau. Slow voltage-gated Ca2+ channels open allowing Ca2+ to enter, while fast K+ channels close. The combination of Ca2+ influx and decreased K+ efflux creates a flat plateau, lengthening the action potential to 200+ msec.

q: Describe Phase 3 of an action potential in cardiac contractile cells.
a: Phase 3 is rapid repolarization. The plateau ends when Ca2+ channels close, and slow K+ channels open allowing rapid K+ efflux, returning the cell to the resting potential of -90 mV.

q: Explain the refractory period in cardiac muscle and its relationship to tetanus.
a: The extended plateau phase means the refractory period lasts almost as long as the entire muscle twitch, so the cell almost completely relaxes by the time a second action potential can fire, preventing summation and the fatal condition of tetanus.

q: Describe the resting membrane state of myocardial autorhythmic cells.
a: They have an unstable membrane potential called a pacemaker potential that starts at -60 mV and slowly drifts up toward threshold.

q: What are I_f channels (funny channels)?
a: Channels belonging to the HCN family that open at -60 mV and are permeable to both K+ and Na+.

q: Explain the role of I_f channels and ion movement in creating the pacemaker potential.
a: Na+ influx exceeds K+ efflux through the I_f channels, creating a net influx of positive charge that slowly depolarizes the cell. As the cell becomes more positive, I_f channels gradually close.

q: What causes the continuation of steady depolarization toward threshold in autorhythmic cells after I_f channels close?
a: One set of Ca2+ channels opens, allowing an influx of Ca2+.

q: Describe the depolarization phase of an action potential in myocardial autorhythmic cells.
a: When threshold is reached, a different subtype of voltage-gated Ca2+ channels opens, and Ca2+ rushes in, creating the steep depolarization phase.

q: Describe the repolarization phase of an action potential in myocardial autorhythmic cells.
a: At the peak, Ca2+ channels close and slow K+ channels open. The efflux of K+ repolarizes the cell back to -60 mV.

q: Compare the ions responsible for the steep depolarization phase in contractile cells versus autorhythmic cells.
a: In contractile cells, steep depolarization is caused by Na+ influx, whereas in autorhythmic cells, it is caused by Ca2+ influx.

q: Describe the location and function of the sinoatrial node (SA node).
a: Located in the right atrium; it functions as the main pacemaker by firing an action potential.

q: Describe the location and function of the internodal pathway.
a: Connects the SA node to the AV node; functions to rapidly spread the electrical signal across the atria.

q: Describe the location and function of the atrioventricular node (AV node).
a: Located on the floor of the right atrium; it slows down transmission of the action potential (AV node delay) to allow atria to complete their contraction before ventricles begin, serving as the only electrical gateway to the ventricles.

q: Describe the location and function of the atrioventricular bundle (AV bundle / Bundle of His).
a: Located in the ventricular septum; functions to transmit signals down the septum.

q: Describe the location and function of the bundle branches.
a: Split left and right from the AV bundle; functions to continue downward to the apex of the heart.

q: Describe the location and function of the Purkinje fibers.
a: Spreading outward from the apex among contractile cells; specialized conducting cells that transmit electrical signals very rapidly to ensure near simultaneous contraction of apex cells.

q: Define diastole.
a: The time during which cardiac muscle relaxes.

q: Define systole.
a: The time during which cardiac muscle contracts.

q: Explain the Late Diastole phase of the cardiac cycle.
a: Both atria and ventricles are relaxed, AV valves are open, and blood flows passively by gravity from veins into atria and then into ventricles.

q: Explain the Atrial Systole phase of the cardiac cycle.
a: Atria contract following the P wave, forcing the last 20% of blood into the ventricles.

q: Define end-diastolic volume (EDV).
a: The maximum volume of blood the ventricles contain at the end of relaxation/filling, averaging about 135 mL.

q: Explain the Isovolumic Ventricular Contraction phase of the cardiac cycle.
a: Ventricular systole begins, pressure in ventricles rises to force AV valves closed, and ventricles contract isometrically without changing volume because semilunar valves remain closed.

q: Define the first heart sound (S1).
a: The lub sound created by vibrations following the closure of the AV valves.

q: Explain the Ventricular Ejection phase of the cardiac cycle.
a: Ventricular pressure exceeds arterial pressure in the aorta/pulmonary trunk, causing semilunar valves to open and blood to be ejected.

q: Define end-systolic volume (ESV).
a: The minimum volume of blood left in the ventricle at the end of contraction, averaging about 65 mL.

q: Explain the Isovolumic Ventricular Relaxation phase of the cardiac cycle.
a: Ventricles begin to relax and repolarize, pressure decreases, and blood flows backward from arteries to snap the semilunar valves closed, keeping the ventricles as sealed chambers until pressure drops below atrial pressure.

q: Define the second heart sound (S2).
a: The dup sound created by vibrations from semilunar valve closure.

q: Describe the P wave of an ECG.
a: Represents atrial depolarization.

q: Describe the QRS complex of an ECG.
a: Represents the progressive wave of ventricular depolarization, with atrial repolarization hidden within the complex.

q: Describe the T wave of an ECG.
a: Represents ventricular repolarization.

q: Describe the P-R segment of an ECG.
a: Represents conduction through the AV node and AV bundle (AV node delay), and this is where atrial contraction occurs.

q: Define Stroke Volume (SV) and provide its equation.
a: The amount of blood pumped by one ventricle during one contraction. Equation: SV = EDV - ESV.

q: Define Ejection Fraction and provide its equation.
a: The percentage of EDV ejected with one contraction. Equation: Ejection Fraction = SV / EDV.

q: Define Cardiac Output (CO) and provide its equation.
a: The volume of blood pumped by one ventricle in a given period of time, serving as an indicator of total blood flow through the body. Equation: CO = Heart Rate x Stroke Volume.

q: Explain the cellular mechanism by which the parasympathetic branch slows heart rate.
a: Parasympathetic neurons release ACh which binds to muscarinic cholinergic receptors on autorhythmic cells, increasing K+ permeability to hyperpolarize the cell, and decreasing Ca2+ permeability to slow the rate of pacemaker depolarization.

q: Explain the cellular mechanism by which the sympathetic branch speeds heart rate.
a: Sympathetic neurons release Norepinephrine, which binds to beta-1-adrenergic receptors on autorhythmic cells, activating a cAMP second messenger system to keep I_f channels open longer, increasing Na+ and Ca2+ influx.

q: END
a: END
